Serving HTTP on 0.0.0.0 port 8080 (http://0.0.0.0:8080/) ...

docs.title: Step 2 — Verify the web server works
docs.text:
Open your browser and visit your Tailscale IP on port 8080:

http://100.x.x.x:8080

Replace 100.x.x.x with your actual Tailscale IP from tailscale ip. You should see:

Zero Trust Lab - Service Running on Port 8080

docs.title: Step 3 — View the default Tailscale ACL
docs.text:
Open your browser and go to:

https://login.tailscale.com/admin/acls

You will see the default ACL:

{
  "grants": [
    {
      "src": ["*"],
      "dst": ["*"],
      "ip": ["*"]
    }
  ]
}

What this means

Everyone can reach everything on every port. This is the equivalent of having no security at all — classic perimeter thinking.

docs.title: Step 4 — Write the micro-segmentation ACL
docs.text:
Update the ACL to allow access on port 8080 only:

{
  "grants": [
    {
      "src": ["*"],
      "dst": ["*"],
      "ip": ["8080"]
    }
  ],
  "ssh": [
    {
      "action": "check",
      "src": ["autogroup:member"],
      "dst": ["autogroup:self"],
      "users": ["autogroup:nonroot", "root"]
    }
  ]
}

Click Save.

Part Meaning "src": ["*"] From any authenticated user on the tailnet "dst": ["*"] To any device on the tailnet "ip": ["8080"] But ONLY on port 8080 Everything else Implicitly blocked

Key concept

In Zero Trust, the default is DENY. You explicitly allow only what is needed. Everything not mentioned is automatically blocked.

docs.title: Step 5 — Test port 8080 is allowed
docs.text:
Visit your web server again via Tailscale IP:

http://100.x.x.x:8080

It should still load successfully.

docs.title: Step 6 — Test other ports are blocked
docs.text:
Open a new terminal and run:

curl -v http://100.x.x.x:9090 --max-time 5

Expected output:

* Trying 100.x.x.x:9090...
* Connection timed out after 5001 milliseconds
* Closing connection 0
curl: (28) Connection timed out after 5000 milliseconds

The timeout is a success

A timeout means the connection never went through. The ACL rule is working exactly as intended.

docs.title: What You Just Proved
docs.text:
Port Result Reason 8080 Accessible Explicitly allowed in ACL 9090 Blocked Not in ACL, default deny All others Blocked Not in ACL, default deny

🎉 Milestone 2 Complete! Even authenticated users on your private network can only reach the specific service you explicitly allowed. Lateral movement is now impossible.

docs.title: What This Milestone Covers
docs.text:
Even authenticated users with network access should only be able to do exactly what their job requires — nothing more. This is the Principle of Least Privilege.

In this milestone we will create a junior-admin user representing a low-privilege employee, configure a sudoers rule giving them exactly one permission, and prove they can restart the ssh service but cannot read sensitive files or install software.

docs.title: Step 1 — Create the junior-admin user
docs.text:
sudo useradd -m -s /bin/bash junior-admin

Set a password:

sudo passwd junior-admin

Use a simple password like Junior@123 for this lab environment.

Verify the user was created:

cat /etc/passwd | grep junior-admin

Expected output:

junior-admin:x:1001:1001::/home/junior-admin:/bin/bash

docs.title: Step 2 — Understand the sudoers file
docs.text:
The /etc/sudoers file controls who can run which commands as root. The syntax is:

username    ALL=(ALL)    NOPASSWD: /path/to/command
Part Meaning username Which user this rule applies to ALL= On any machine (ALL) Can run as any user NOPASSWD: Without entering a password /path/to/command ONLY this specific command

Always use visudo

Never edit /etc/sudoers directly with a text editor. visudo checks for syntax errors before saving — a syntax error in sudoers can lock you out of sudo completely.

docs.title: Step 3 — Add the least privilege rule
docs.text:
Open the sudoers file safely:

sudo visudo

Scroll to the very bottom and add this line:

junior-admin ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD: /usr/sbin/service ssh restart

Save and exit:

  • Nano: Ctrl + X → Y → Enter
  • Vim: Esc → :wq → Enter

docs.title: Step 4 — Switch to junior-admin and test
docs.text:
Switch to the junior-admin user:

su - junior-admin

Your prompt should change to:

junior-admin@kali:~$

Test 1 — junior-admin CAN restart ssh

sudo service ssh restart && service ssh status

Expected output:

● ssh.service - OpenBSD Secure Shell server
     Loaded: loaded (/lib/systemd/system/ssh.service)
     Active: active (running) since ...

Test 2 — junior-admin CANNOT read sensitive files

sudo cat /etc/shadow

Expected output:

Sorry, user junior-admin is not allowed to execute
'/usr/bin/cat /etc/shadow' as root on kali.

Test 3 — junior-admin CANNOT install software

sudo apt update

Expected output:

Sorry, user junior-admin is not allowed to execute
'/usr/bin/apt update' as root on kali.

docs.title: Step 5 — Switch back to your main user
docs.text:
exit

Your prompt should return to:

kali@kali:~$

docs.title: What You Just Proved
docs.text:
Action User Result Reason sudo service ssh restart junior-admin Allowed Explicitly in sudoers rule sudo cat /etc/shadow junior-admin Blocked Not in sudoers rule sudo apt update junior-admin Blocked Not in sudoers rule

🎉 Milestone 3 Complete! A junior employee can do exactly their job — restart a service when needed — but cannot access sensitive data or make system-wide changes.

docs.title: What This Milestone Covers
docs.text:
Security analysts deal with thousands of log lines every day. Manually reading every line is slow, error-prone, and exhausting. Generative AI can act as a co-pilot — reading logs instantly, explaining what happened, and flagging suspicious activity in plain English.

In this milestone we will read the authentication logs from our lab session, paste them into Claude or ChatGPT, and ask the AI to analyse what security events occurred.

This is a real technique

AI doesn't replace the analyst — it speeds up the investigation so the analyst can focus on decisions, not manual log reading. This is how modern security teams work today.

docs.title: Step 1 — View your authentication logs
docs.text:
On Kali Linux, authentication events are stored in the systemd journal. Run this to see all sudo-related activity from the last hour:

sudo journalctl _COMM=sudo --since "1 hour ago"

You will see entries recording every sudo command that was run during your lab — including the junior-admin permission grants and denials.

Why no auth.log?

Kali Linux uses systemd-journald instead of the traditional auth.log file. The journalctl command is the modern equivalent and contains the same information.

docs.title: Step 2 — Copy the log output
docs.text:
Run the command again and select all the output text with your mouse. Copy it.

sudo journalctl _COMM=sudo --since "1 hour ago"

docs.title: Step 3 — Open Claude or ChatGPT
docs.text: Open a new browser tab and go to https://claude.ai or https://chatgpt.com.

docs.title: Step 4 — Paste this prompt
docs.text:
Copy this entire prompt, paste it into the AI chat, then paste your log lines after it:

You are a security analyst. I am a Junior Security Analyst
learning about Zero Trust Architecture.

Please analyse the following Linux authentication logs from
my lab session and explain:
1. What security events occurred
2. Which events show successful privilege use
3. Which events show blocked/denied access attempts
4. What this tells us about the Principle of Least Privilege
5. Are there any suspicious or concerning entries?

Here are the logs:

[PASTE YOUR LOG LINES HERE]

docs.title: Step 5 — Review the AI's response
docs.text: The AI will analyse your logs and explain which commands were run with sudo, which were allowed vs denied, what the pattern tells us about access control, and any anomalies.

docs.title: What good AI log analysis looks like
docs.text: Log entry type What it means COMMAND=/usr/sbin/service ssh restart junior-admin restarted ssh — allowed by sudoers command not allowed junior-admin tried something outside their permissions sudo: pam_unix(sudo:auth) A sudo authentication event occurred session opened for user root A sudo command successfully ran as root session closed for user root The sudo session ended

docs.title: Using AI as a Security Co-Pilot — Best Practices
docs.text:
Be specific in your prompts

The more context you give the AI, the better its analysis. Tell it what you were doing, what users were involved, and what you're looking for.

Always verify AI findings

AI can make mistakes. Use it to speed up your analysis but always verify important findings yourself.

Never paste real production logs into public AI tools

In a real company, logs may contain sensitive information. Use private/enterprise AI tools or anonymise logs before pasting.

Iterate on your prompts

If the first response isn't detailed enough, ask follow-up questions like "Can you explain the denied entries in more detail?" or "What would an attacker learn from these logs?"

docs.title: What You Just Proved
docs.text:
Traditional approach AI-assisted approach Manually read hundreds of log lines AI reads and summarises instantly Easy to miss patterns AI identifies patterns across all entries Time consuming Results in seconds Requires deep log expertise AI explains in plain English

🎉 Milestone 4 Complete! You have used AI as a real security tool — not just a chatbot.

docs.title: Trust Boundary Diagram
docs.text:
This diagram shows how your Zero Trust setup controls access at every layer — from network identity all the way down to individual user permissions.

Boundary 1 — Network layer (Tailscale) No one can reach your machine without first authenticating with a verified identity. IP addresses mean nothing here.

Boundary 2 — Service layer (ACL rules) Even authenticated users can only reach port 8080. All other ports are silently blocked regardless of who is asking.

Boundary 3 — Privilege layer (sudoers) Even users with network access can only perform the specific actions their role requires. Everything else is denied.

Defence in depth

Multiple independent layers of security. An attacker would need to break through all three layers, not just one.

docs.title: Test Your Understanding
docs.text:
Q1. What is the main difference between Traditional Perimeter Security and Zero Trust Architecture? Traditional Perimeter Security trusts everyone inside the network — once you're past the firewall, you can reach anything. Zero Trust trusts no one by default — every access request must be verified regardless of where it comes from.

Q2. In your Tailscale ACL, what happens to traffic on port 443 (HTTPS)? It is blocked. In Zero Trust, the default is DENY. Only port 8080 was explicitly allowed in the ACL. Any port not mentioned is automatically blocked — including port 443.

Q3. Why did we use visudo instead of directly editing /etc/sudoers with a text editor? visudo checks the sudoers file for syntax errors before saving. A syntax error in /etc/sudoers can completely lock you out of sudo — meaning you cannot run any admin commands. visudo prevents this by validating the file first.

Q4. What would happen if junior-admin tried to run sudo reboot? It would be blocked. The sudoers rule only allows junior-admin to run /usr/sbin/service ssh restart. Any other command — including sudo reboot — would return "Sorry, user junior-admin is not allowed to execute this command."

Q5. Why should you never paste real production logs into a public AI tool like ChatGPT? Production logs may contain sensitive information such as usernames, IP addresses, hostnames, service names, and access patterns. Pasting these into a public AI tool means that data leaves your organisation and could be exposed in a data breach or used to train AI models. Always use enterprise AI tools or anonymise logs before analysis.

Q6. What does the --operator=$USER flag do in sudo tailscale up --operator=$USER? It grants your normal (non-root) user the ability to control Tailscale without needing sudo every time. Without this flag, only root can run Tailscale commands. $USER automatically fills in your current username.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• NIST SP 800-207 — Zero Trust Architecture
  • Tailscale Documentation
  • Tailscale ACL Reference
  • Linux sudoers Manual
  • MITRE ATT&CK — Lateral Movement

docs.title: Learning Outcomes
docs.text:
Having completed this lab, you have:

  • Installed Tailscale and registered a device to establish identity-based network access
  • Written ACL rules to enforce micro-segmentation, explicitly allowing only port 8080 and denying all other traffic by default
  • Created a restricted junior-admin sudoers entry applying the Principle of Least Privilege
  • Verified that commands outside the allowed scope are blocked for the restricted user
  • Used Generative AI to analyse authentication logs and identify suspicious access patterns
  • Traced the full Zero Trust boundary across network identity, micro-segmentation, and privilege layers

docs.title: Zero Trust & Identity Lab
docs.text: Beginner Friendly • 2 Hours • Hands-on Security Lab

docs.title: Lab Overview
docs.text:
This lab demonstrates how modern Zero Trust Architecture replaces traditional perimeter-based security using:

  • Identity-based networking
  • Micro-segmentation
  • Least privilege access control
  • AI-assisted log analysis

By the end of this lab, you will build a functioning Zero Trust environment using Linux and Tailscale.

docs.title: Learning Objectives
docs.text:
After completing this lab, you will be able to:

  • Configure identity-based networking with Tailscale
  • Implement micro-segmentation using ACLs
  • Restrict user privileges using Linux sudoers
  • Analyse authentication logs with Generative AI
  • Understand Zero Trust concepts from NIST SP 800-207

docs.title: Technologies Used

docs.title: Traditional Security vs Zero Trust
docs.text: Traditional Perimeter Security Zero Trust Architecture Trust users inside network Trust nobody by default IP-based access Identity-based access Broad permissions Least privilege Easy lateral movement Micro-segmentation Single trust boundary Continuous verification

docs.title: Core Principle
docs.text:
Quote

"Never Trust. Always Verify."

In Zero Trust: - Every request must be authenticated - Every action must be authorised - Every connection is continuously validated

docs.title: Lab Architecture
docs.text:
graph TD

    User[Authenticated User]
    --> Tailscale[Tailscale Identity Network]

    Tailscale --> ACL[ACL Rules]

    ACL --> Web8080[Port 8080 Allowed]
    ACL --> Blocked[All Other Ports Blocked]

    Web8080 --> RBAC[Linux sudoers RBAC]

    RBAC --> RestartSSH[Can Restart SSH]
    RBAC --> Denied[Cannot Read Sensitive Files]

    RBAC --> Logs[Authentication Logs]

    Logs --> AI[ChatGPT / Claude Analysis]

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
Requirement Details OS Kali Linux / Ubuntu / Debian Account GitHub or Google Network Internet access Skill Level Beginner Time Required ~2 hours

Warning

Use a personal GitHub or Google account for Tailscale authentication.

docs.title: Goal
docs.text: Replace traditional IP-based trust with identity-based networking using Tailscale.

docs.title: Step 1 — Update Your System
docs.text:
sudo apt update
sudo apt upgrade -y

Expected output:

Reading package lists... Done
All packages are up to date.

docs.title: Step 2 — Enable IP Forwarding
docs.text:
echo 'net.ipv4.ip_forward = 1' | sudo tee -a /etc/sysctl.conf

sudo sysctl -p

Expected output:

net.ipv4.ip_forward = 1

Tip

IP forwarding allows Linux to route packets between interfaces.

docs.title: Step 3 — Install Tailscale
docs.text:
curl -fsSL https://tailscale.com/install.sh | sh

Expected output:

Installation complete!

docs.title: Step 4 — Authenticate with Your Identity
docs.text:
sudo tailscale up --operator=$USER

Tailscale will generate a login URL.

Open it in your browser and authenticate using: - GitHub - Google

docs.title: Step 5 — Verify Connection
docs.text:
tailscale status

tailscale ip

Expected output:

100.x.x.x

Success

Your machine is now connected to an identity-based network.

docs.title: What You Achieved
docs.text: Before After IP-based trust Identity-based trust Anyone with IP can connect Only authenticated users can connect Limited visibility Identity-linked activity logging

docs.title: Goal
docs.text: Allow access ONLY to port 8080 while blocking all other lateral movement.

docs.title: Step 1 — Create a Web Server
docs.text:
mkdir -p ~/lab-server

echo "<h1>Zero Trust Lab</h1>" > ~/lab-server/index.html

docs.title: Step 2 — Start the Server
docs.text:
cd ~/lab-server

python3 -m http.server 8080

Expected output:

Serving HTTP on 0.0.0.0 port 8080

docs.title: Step 3 — Test the Web Service
docs.text:
Open:

http://100.x.x.x:8080

You should see:

Zero Trust Lab

docs.title: Step 4 — Configure Tailscale ACLs
docs.text:
Go to the Tailscale ACL dashboard and replace the default ACL with:

{
  "grants": [
    {
      "src": ["*"],
      "dst": ["*"],
      "ip": ["8080"]
    }
  ]
}

docs.title: ACL Explanation
docs.text:
ACL Component Meaning "src" Source user "dst" Destination device "ip" Allowed port Unlisted ports Blocked automatically

Info

Zero Trust follows a default DENY model.

docs.title: Step 5 — Verify Allowed Port
docs.text:
curl http://100.x.x.x:8080

Expected output:

<h1>Zero Trust Lab</h1>

docs.title: Step 6 — Verify Blocked Port
docs.text:
curl -v http://100.x.x.x:9090 --max-time 5

Expected output:

Connection timed out

Success

Micro-segmentation is now enforced.

docs.title: What You Achieved
docs.text: Port Result 8080 Allowed 9090 Blocked Other Ports Blocked

docs.title: Goal
docs.text: Allow a junior administrator to restart SSH — but nothing else.

docs.title: Step 1 — Create User
docs.text:
sudo useradd -m -s /bin/bash junior-admin

Set password:

sudo passwd junior-admin

docs.title: Step 2 — Edit sudoers Safely
docs.text:
sudo visudo

Add:

junior-admin ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD: /usr/sbin/service ssh restart

Warning

Always use visudo instead of directly editing /etc/sudoers.

docs.title: Step 3 — Switch User
docs.text: su - junior-admin

docs.title: Step 4 — Test Allowed Command
docs.text:
sudo service ssh restart

Expected: - Command succeeds

docs.title: Step 5 — Test Blocked Command
docs.text:
sudo cat /etc/shadow

Expected output:

Sorry, user junior-admin is not allowed to execute this command.

docs.title: Step 6 — Test Package Installation Block
docs.text:
sudo apt update

Expected: - Access denied

Success

Least privilege is now enforced.

docs.title: What You Achieved
docs.text: Command Result Restart SSH Allowed Read /etc/shadow Blocked Install packages Blocked

docs.title: Milestone 4 — Generative AI as Security Co-pilot
Goal
docs.text: Use AI to analyse Linux authentication logs.

docs.title: Step 1 — View Authentication Logs
docs.text: sudo journalctl _COMM=sudo --since "1 hour ago"

docs.title: Step 2 — Copy the Logs
docs.text: Copy the command output.

docs.title: Step 3 — Open ChatGPT or Claude
docs.text:
Paste the following prompt:

You are a security analyst.

Please analyse these Linux authentication logs and explain:

1. Successful privilege usage
2. Denied access attempts
3. Suspicious activity
4. Least privilege enforcement
5. Any security concerns

Logs:

[PASTE LOGS HERE]

docs.title: Expected AI Analysis
docs.text:
The AI should identify: - Successful sudo actions - Blocked commands - User privilege boundaries - Security patterns - Potential concerns

Warning

Never upload real production logs into public AI systems.

docs.title: Why AI Helps Security Teams
docs.text: Traditional Analysis AI-Assisted Analysis Manual review Automated summarisation Time consuming Faster investigations Hard to identify patterns Pattern recognition Requires deep expertise Plain-English explanations

docs.title: Three Layers of Protection
docs.text:
Layer 1 — IdentityLayer 2 — ACL EnforcementLayer 3 — Least Privilege

Users must authenticate before accessing the network.

Only explicitly allowed services are reachable.

Users can perform only approved actions.

docs.title: Check Your Understanding
docs.text:
Why is Zero Trust safer than perimeter security?

Zero Trust validates every request regardless of network location.

Why was port 9090 blocked?

Only port 8080 was explicitly allowed in the ACL.

Why use visudo?

visudo validates syntax before saving changes.

What would happen if junior-admin ran sudo reboot?

The action would be denied because it is not allowed in sudoers.

Why should production logs not be pasted into public AI tools?

Logs may contain sensitive or confidential organisational data.

docs.title: Final Summary
docs.text: Congratulations! You successfully built a functioning Zero Trust lab environment.
